fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.

Let f , g \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H\colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx. We shall write 1_{f}\colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{f}x:\equiv \mathsf {refl}.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{B} and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{A}, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon , we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.

An inverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f\circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with an inverse as an invertible function.
A function f\colon A\to B is called an embedding when for any x,y:A the action on identifications \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x=y\to fx=fy is invertible.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.
Any embedding f\colon A\to B is propositional.

We must check that for every b:B, the fiber of f at b is a proposition. Fixing u_0,u_1:\mathsf {fiber}\,f\,b we must check that u_0=u_1. We let \mathopen {}\left \lparen a_0,p_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} \equiv u_0 and \mathopen {}\left \lparen a_1,p_1\right \rparen \mathclose {} \equiv u_1 so that we have p_0\colon fa_0=b and p_1\colon fa_1=b. Invoking the path object structure on fibers (), it suffices to exhibit the following data:
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\colon B\to A,\epsilon \colon f\circ g =1_{B}\right \rparen \mathclose {} be a section of a function f\colon A\to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} P &:\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ P &\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} =_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.
Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via and .
Let f\colon A\to B be a retraction of some s\colon B\to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of -\circ f at 1_{A}.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.

fr:title.text: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{A}.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a section.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a retraction r\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r\colon r\circ f\sim 1_{A} exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a section s\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s\colon f\circ s\sim 1_{B} exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
A function f\colon A\to B is called surjective when for any b\colon B, the fiber of f over b is inhabited.

Given f\colon A\to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen f-=b\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord {\vartriangle }A:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx=b\right \rparen \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\phi \right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx=b\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,\psi \right \rparen \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \phi = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \psi .

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.
